radPrimerImageCoverageBySTATdx.evidenceBasis: actual image_evidence file visual review; source IDs and SHA-256 hashes were checked and are not shared, so captions alone were not used
radPrimerImageCoverageBySTATdx.rationale: STATdx SDX-01 is the same visual image/screenshot/content as RP-01: same newborn skull/scalp-layer graphic. Use the STATdx copy as the selected primary representative and keep the RadPrimer copy only for audit/recovery.

sourceAttributionRules: Use [RadPrimer], [STATdx], and [Both] when source distinction matters.
Use clean human-facing image labels in packageText, for example RadPrimer image 5 or STATdx image 4.
Keep RP-05 and SDX-04 in registry, manifest, filenames, and traceability fields rather than duplicating them in narrative prose.

textToKeep: Use metadata.json canonicalHierarchy exactly for package routing.
Use RadPrimer as the canonical hierarchy/backbone for Scalp and Skull Injuries.
Use STATdx supplemental depth and image examples for cephalohematoma, subgaleal hematoma, skull fracture patterns, vascular-channel risk, and complications.
downweightOrSkip: Do not use STATdx breadcrumb for deck routing; metadata.json canonicalHierarchy remains authoritative.
Do not generate cards or a lecture from this package until the user explicitly asks.
Do not archive images from caption similarity alone; exact duplicate calls here are based on actual staged image evidence visual review.

imageDownloadPlan.caption: Graphic shows the skull of a newborn, including the anterior fontanelle, coronal, metopic, and sagittal sutures. Cephalohematoma is subperiosteal, focal, and limited by sutures. Subgaleal hematoma is under the scalp aponeurosis, much more extensive, and not bounded by sutures.
Bone CT in a newborn with a traumatic delivery shows a linear skull fracture and cephalohematoma overlying the parietal bone. Note that the cephalohematoma does not cross the sagittal suture .
NECT in the same patient shows the cephalohematoma . Note the presence of a small epidural hematoma .

Axial NECT shows an infant with a large subgaleal hematoma. In contrast to cephalohematoma, this subgaleal hematoma crosses the midline and extends nearly all the way around the entire head. Large subgaleal hematomas in infants can cause life-threatening blood loss.

Autopsied skull shows fatal trauma with exo- (L) and endocranial views (R). A linear fracture extends into the superior sagittal suture , causing diastasis and a subgaleal hematoma that crosses the midline.
Bone CT through the top of the calvarium in a patient with severe head injury shows linear skull fractures extending into and widening the sagittal suture, causing a diastatic fracture .

3D-shaded surface display (SSD) in a patient with multiple linear and diastatic skull fractures shows utility of SSDs in depicting complex fracture anatomy. Note slight depression of the fractured parietooccipital calvarium that was appreciated only on this SSD.

Axial NECT [brain (L) and bone (R) windows] in a young male who was hit with a hammer shows a comminuted, depressed skull fracture . The brain under the fracture shows only a small cortical contusion . The patient's GCS was 15.
Axial NECT in a patient with a "growing" skull fracture shows widened fracture with scalloped margins .
Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows a lobulated CSF collection filling the "growing" fracture. CSF pulsations have widened and scalloped the calvarium.

Graphic shows the skull of a newborn, including the anterior fontanelle, coronal, metopic, and sagittal sutures. Cephalohematoma is subperiosteal, focal, and limited by sutures. Subgaleal hematoma is under the scalp aponeurosis, much more extensive, and not bounded by sutures.
Bone CT in a newborn with a traumatic delivery shows a linear skull fracture and a cephalohematoma overlying the parietal bone. Note that the cephalohematoma does not cross the sagittal suture .
NECT in the same patient shows the cephalohematoma . Note the presence of a small intracranial epidural hematoma .

Axial NECT shows an infant with a large subgaleal hematoma. In contrast to cephalohematoma, this subgaleal hematoma crosses the midline and extends nearly all the way around the entire head. Large subgaleal hematomas in infants can cause life-threatening blood loss.

Autopsied skull shows fatal trauma with exo- (L) & endocranial views (R). A linear fracture extends into the superior sagittal suture , causing diastasis & a subgaleal hematoma that the crosses midline.

Coronal NECT shows a subgaleal hematoma crossing the midline sagittal suture. Skull base & facial fractures & a left frontal skull fracture are better seen on bone windows. Note the pneumocephalus , indicating a fracture through a paranasal sinus through a paranasal sinus or the calvarium.
Axial bone CT in a 48-year-old trauma patient shows a left temporal bone skull fracture that also involves the greater wing of the sphenoid .

Axial NECT in the same patient shows an associated epidural hematoma in the anterior middle cranial fossa. Skull fractures are commonly associated with epidural hematomas. Note the contrecoup subarachnoid hemorrhage in the right temporal region.
Axial NECT in a patient with a "growing" skull fracture shows a widened fracture with scalloped margins .

Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows a lobulated CSF collection filling the "growing" fracture. CSF pulsations have widened and scalloped the calvarium. "Growing" skull fractures are difficult to detect in the acute stage and are often identified on follow-up imaging as a progressively widening, unhealing fracture. On CT, they are lucent lesions with rounded, scalloped margins.
Axial NECT in a newborn with difficult vacuum-assisted delivery shows a small right and a large left cephalohematoma. Neither crosses the midline.
Coronal NECT in the same patient shows the small right and large left cephalohematomas. The elevated periosteum clearly separates the two blood collections.
Sagittal NECT shows the left parietal cephalohematoma does not cross the coronal suture .
Axial T2 MR in an abused infant shows a very large mixed acute/subacute subgaleal hematoma crossing the sutures and extending over the face/orbits.
More cephalad axial T2 MR shows the mixed-age subgaleal hematoma crossing the midline.

Axial NECT (left) shows severe scalp laceration with a combination of elevated and depressed skull fractures. Axial bone CT (right) shows that the elevated fracture is literally "hinged" away from the calvarium.
Axial NECT shows a large subgaleal hematoma crossing the sagittal suture. Hyperdense vertex hematoma also crosses the midline, suggesting that the blood is in the epidural space.

Axial bone CT shows a diastatic fracture of the sagittal suture . A very large subgaleal hematoma extends around the entire circumference of the skull. The superior sagittal sinus is torn, and the intracranial blood seen on soft tissue windows was a vertex venous epidural hematoma.
Axial NECT in a patient with a blow to the right side of the head shows a mildly depressed fracture . Note that the brain under the fracture appears quite normal.

Axial NECT with bone algorithm reconstruction and wide window display in the same patient shows a depressed, comminuted skull fracture involving the right squamous temporal bone. Note the subcutaneous air and soft tissue hematoma overlying the fracture.
Axial NECT in a patient who was hit in the head shows an extensively comminuted, depressed skull fracture . Note the large subgaleal hematoma completely surrounding the entire skull.
Bone CT through the top of the calvarium in a patient with severe head injury shows linear skull fractures extending into and widening the sagittal suture, causing a diastatic fracture .

Axial NECT [brain (L) and bone (R) windows] in a young male who was hit with a hammer shows a comminuted, depressed skull fracture . The brain under the fracture shows only a small cortical contusion . The patient's GCS was 15.

Axial graphic depicts different basilar skull fractures crossing the petrous apex and clivus as well as extending into the jugular foramen and carotid canal . The potential for vascular injury in such fractures is high.

3D shaded surface display in a patient with multiple linear and diastatic skull fractures shows utility of SSDs in depicting complex fracture anatomy. Note slight depression of the fractured parietooccipital calvarium that was appreciated only on this SSD.

Axial bone CT shows a subgaleal hematoma crossing the midline sagittal suture. A left frontal skull fracture is present. Note the pneumocephalus . Subgaleal hematomas are under the scalp aponeurosis, much more extensive, and not bounded by sutures. They are often associated with skull fractures in trauma patients.

sourceSelectionPlan.imageCurationPolicy: Exact duplicates may be archived; near duplicates and conceptual replacements remain selected as recognition reinforcement unless visually identical or unusable. In this bundle every RadPrimer image is visually covered by an exact STATdx duplicate, so RadPrimer copies are archiveOptionalDuplicate and STATdx copies are the selected primary set.
sourceSelectionPlan.duplicateEvidencePolicy: ExactDuplicate decisions require actual image_evidence files, same stable source image ID, image hashes, or explicit manual/source confirmation. For this bundle, source IDs and SHA-256 hashes are different across sources, but direct visual review of the staged evidence files supports exact duplicate status for the listed pairs. Caption-only similarity was not used.

caseClusterGuardrails.policy: Treat same-patient, follow-up, adjacent-slice, comparison-view, procedure, and time-lapse groups as atomic teaching clusters unless an explicit split is documented.

clusterSelections.reason: same newborn/same-patient cephalohematoma and intracranial epidural hematoma teaching pair
same-patient CT/MR growing skull fracture pair
same infant mixed-age subgaleal hematoma T2 cluster
subgaleal hematoma, sagittal suture diastasis, and superior sagittal sinus injury cluster
same-patient depressed temporal fracture brain/bone window companion pair
same-patient temporal bone fracture with epidural hematoma/contrecoup SAH companion pair

caseClusterGuardrails.note: No source case cluster was intentionally split; exact duplicate RadPrimer clusters were replaced by equivalent STATdx cluster members where available.

generatorInstructions: Use this as the fused RadPrimer + STATdx master source for Scalp and Skull Injuries.
Cite source contributions as [RadPrimer], [STATdx], or [Both] and use clean labels such as STATdx image 11 in prose.
Download/use only selectedPrimaryImageIds by default; archiveOptionalImageIds are for duplicate recovery or complete-source audit only.
